ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No precise SNOMED CT code identified for UHT flavoured milk as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed dairy beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve UHT flavoured milk carton of 200 ml (approximately 207 g) is the typical portion consumed by children and adults as a snack or with a meal.

nutrient_source.title: UHT Flavoured Milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for UHT flavoured milk (cr_id F01030015). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole milk (NDB 01077) and adjusted proportionally for flavoured variant, as HPB does not provide these specific values. Adaptations are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for flavoured milk (chocolate milk, low-fat). This is used as the closest defensible analogous food for UHT flavoured milk. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (31) × carbohydrate per serving (10.5 g for 207 g serving) / 100 = 3.3. The input record cited GI 31 from an 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.333; this has been validated against the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flavoured milk and is retained as an analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a sweetened dairy beverage; sodium is low (44 mg/100 g) but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid UHT flavoured milk as a regular beverage due to its added sugar content (5.1 g/100 g); plain unsweetened UHT milk is preferred to eliminate the sugar-driven blood pressure risk identified in AHA 2018 guidance on sugar-sweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened dairy beverage with 5.1 g added sugar per 100 g, zero dietary fibre, and rapid liquid glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: UHT flavoured milk qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the absence of dietary fibre and the liquid form accelerate postprandial glucose rise — plain unsweetened milk or water should be substituted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) and added sugar (5.1 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk when consumed regularly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, UHT flavoured milk should be limited due to its combined saturated fat and added sugar load; switching to plain low-fat or skimmed UHT milk reduces both saturated fat and sugar exposure while preserving calcium and protein benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (~120 mg/100 g) and protein (3.38 g/100 g) support bone protection; saturated fat below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: UHT flavoured milk may be consumed in moderation by RA patients for its calcium and protein content, but plain milk is preferred to avoid the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: UHT flavoured milk is a shelf-stable dairy beverage produced by ultra-high temperature (UHT) processing, which heats milk to approximately 135–150°C for 2–5 seconds to achieve commercial sterility, followed by aseptic packaging. The product contains whole or standardised milk as its base, with added sugar and flavouring agents such as chocolate, strawberry, or vanilla. Per 100 g, it provides 64 kcal of energy, 3.38 g protein, 3.38 g fat (of which approximately 2.1 g is saturated), and 5.08 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with zero dietary fibre and 44 mg sodium. Calcium content is approximately 120 mg/100 g, making it a meaningful source of dietary calcium. The glycemic index is low (GI 31), consistent with other flavoured milk products, though the absence of fibre and the liquid form mean postprandial glucose response is faster than solid dairy foods. Clinically, UHT flavoured milk is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its added sugar content, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. For cardiovascular health, the combined saturated fat and added sugar load warrants moderation. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenient single-serve snack or school beverage, available in 200 ml cartons. It is vegetarian, halal-suitable, and gluten-free, but contains milk allergens and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in UHT milk?
Is flavoured milk good for children?
What is the GI of chocolate milk?
Is UHT milk the same as fresh milk?
Can I drink flavoured milk if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a carton of flavoured milk?
Is UHT flavoured milk suitable for weight loss?
What is the calcium content of flavoured milk?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01030015, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, cholesterol, and iron supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for whole milk (NDB 01077) and adapted for flavoured variant; these are well-established values for standard whole-milk-based flavoured milk. GI value of 31 validated against University of Sydney GI Database for flavoured milk. Overall confidence is high for core macros and medium for supplemented micronutrients.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.87 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.13 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01077, whole milk) and adapted proportionally for UHT flavoured milk, as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible for standard UHT milk formulations per USDA data). 2. Calcium reported as 0.0 in the input HPB record; this is inconsistent with known dairy composition. Calcium has been corrected to 120 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for whole milk (NDB 01077) and is consistent with typical UHT milk calcium content. Iron reported as 0.03 mg/100 g (trace), corrected from 0.0 in input. 3. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific UHT flavoured milk formulation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F01030015, and USDA SR Legacy values for plain whole milk are not directly transferable to a flavoured/sweetened variant without introducing unacceptable estimation error; unavailability confirmed after consulting both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. 4. Potassium (150 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (13 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01077) and are approximate for this flavoured variant. 5. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; flavoured milk contains no fibre by formulation. 6. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (31) × carbohydrate per 207 g serving (10.52 g) / 100 = 3.3. 7. The input record listed calcium as 0.0 and iron as 0.0 with HPB as source; these have been corrected as noted above. 8. Multilingual local names and aliases have been added for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance, as this product is widely consumed in Singapore across all major language communities. These translations represent the standard descriptive names for UHT flavoured milk in each language.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (31), gi_category (low), glycemic_load_per_serving (3.3), and gi_source all present. GI evidence from University of Sydney GI Database via analogous estimate for flavoured milk, documented in gi_source.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all six hard gates pass and all eight Q criteria score 1/1. Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (F01030015). Supplemented nutrients from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01077) with documented adaptation. GI from University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for flavoured milk, GI 31). All -1 fields documented in notes with reason and source consulted. Multilingual naming now complete for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. Flat aliases and aliases_by_language are fully consistent. All three required health conditions present with clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full semantic coverage.
